subject: Physics
question_text: Calculate the potential at a point P due to a charge of 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 C located 9 cm away. Hence, obtain the work done in bringing a charge of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 C from infinity to the point P. Does the answer depend on the path along which the charge is brought?
question_html:
1. Calculate the potential at a point P due to a charge of 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 C located 9 cm away.
  2. Hence, obtain the work done in bringing a charge of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 C from infinity to the point P. Does the answer depend on the path along which the charge is brought?
solution_text: Here, q = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 C; r = 9 cm = 0.09 m Potential at point P due to charge q, {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{r}{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{4 \times 10^{-7}}{0.09}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 V The work done in bringing a charge of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 C from infinity to point P, W = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 {tex}\times{/tex} potential at point P = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 J The work done in bringing the charge from infinity to point P does not depend on the path along which the charge is brought because electrostatic forces are conservative forces.
solution_html:
Here, q = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 C; r = 9 cm = 0.09 m

  1. Potential at point P due to charge q,
    {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{r}{/tex}
    = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{4 \times 10^{-7}}{0.09}{/tex}
    = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 V
  2. The work done in bringing a charge of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 C from infinity to point P,
    W = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 {tex}\times{/tex} potential at point P
    = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 104
    = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 J
    The work done in bringing the charge from infinity to point P does not depend on the path along which the charge is brought because electrostatic forces are conservative forces.

subject: Physics
question_text: Two charges 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 C and -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 C are located 15 cm apart. At what point on the line joining the two charges is the electrical potential zero? Take the potential at infinity to be zero.
question_html: Two charges 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 C and -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 C are located 15 cm apart. At what point on the line joining the two charges is the electrical potential zero? Take the potential at infinity to be zero.
solution_text: Here, q A = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 C; q B = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 C; r = 15 cm = 0.15m Let O be the point, where the electric potential is zero due to the two charges as shown in figure. Suppose that the distance AO = x. Then, BO = r - x = 0.15 - x Electric potential at point O due to q A , {tex}V_{A}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q_{A}}{A O}{/tex} = {tex}9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{3 \times 10^{-8}}{x}=\frac{270}{x}{/tex} Electric potential at point O due to q B , {tex}V_{B}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q_{B}}{B O}{/tex} = {tex}9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{\left(-2 \times 10^{-8}\right)}{0 \cdot 15-x}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{180}{0 \cdot 15-x}{/tex} Since the electric potential at point O is zero, we have V A + V B = 0 or {tex}\frac{270}{x}+\left(-\frac{180}{0 \cdot 15-x}\right){/tex} = 0 or {tex}\frac{270}{x}=\frac{180}{0.15-x}{/tex} or x = 0.09m = 9 cm (from charge of 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 C)
solution_html:
Here, qA = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 C; qB = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 C; r = 15 cm = 0.15m
Let O be the point, where the electric potential is zero due to the two charges as shown in figure.

Suppose that the distance AO = x. Then,
BO = r - x = 0.15 - x
Electric potential at point O due to qA,
{tex}V_{A}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q_{A}}{A O}{/tex}
= {tex}9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{3 \times 10^{-8}}{x}=\frac{270}{x}{/tex}
Electric potential at point O due to qB,
{tex}V_{B}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q_{B}}{B O}{/tex}
= {tex}9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{\left(-2 \times 10^{-8}\right)}{0 \cdot 15-x}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{180}{0 \cdot 15-x}{/tex}
Since the electric potential at point O is zero, we have
VA + VB = 0
or {tex}\frac{270}{x}+\left(-\frac{180}{0 \cdot 15-x}\right){/tex} = 0
or {tex}\frac{270}{x}=\frac{180}{0.15-x}{/tex}
or x = 0.09m = 9 cm (from charge of 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 C)

subject: Physics
question_text: Figure (a) and (b) shows the field lines of a single positive and negative charge respectively: (a) (b) Give the sign of the potential difference V P - V Q and V B - V A . Give the sign of the potential energy difference of a small negative charge between the points Q and P; A and B. Give the sign of the work done by the field in moving a small positive charge from point Q to P. Give the sign of the work done by an external agency in moving a small negative charge from point B to A. Does the kinetic energy of a small negative charge increase or decrease in going from point B to A?
question_html:
Figure (a) and (b) shows the field lines of a single positive and negative charge respectively:

(a)  (b) 

  1. Give the sign of the potential difference VP - VQ and VB - VA.
  2. Give the sign of the potential energy difference of a small negative charge between the points Q and P; A and B.
  3. Give the sign of the work done by the field in moving a small positive charge from point Q to P.
  4. Give the sign of the work done by an external agency in moving a small negative charge from point B to A.
  5. Does the kinetic energy of a small negative charge increase or decrease in going from point B to A?
solution_text: In figure (a), as the field is due to a positive charge is given by , V p - V Q > 0 In figure (b), as the field is due to a negative charge is given by , V A - V B < 0 or V B - V A > 0 The potential energy of a negative charge at point Q will be negative and at point P, it will be still more negative. Therefore, (P.E) Q - (P.E) P > 0 sign of potential energy difference of a small negative charge between Q and P is positive. Thus, (P.E) A - (P.E) B > 0 A small positive charge will tend to move from point P to Q and the work done by the field in moving the charge from point P to Q will be positive. Therefore, work done by the electric field in moving a small positive charge from point Q to P will be negative. For the reasons as given in (iii), work done by an external agency in moving a small negative charge from B to A will be positive. As the potential energy of the negative charge increases, the kinetic energy of the negative charge decreases in going from point B to A.
solution_html:
1. In figure (a), as the field is due to a positive charge is given by ,
    Vp - VQ > 0
    In figure (b), as the field is due to a negative charge is given by ,
    VA - VB < 0
    or VB - VA > 0
  2. The potential energy of a negative charge at point Q will be negative and at point P, it will be still more negative. Therefore,
    (P.E)Q - (P.E)P > 0
    sign of potential energy difference of a small negative charge between Q and P is positive. Thus,
    (P.E)A - (P.E)B > 0
  3. A small positive charge will tend to move from point P to Q and the work done by the field in moving the charge from point P to Q will be positive. Therefore, work done by the electric field in moving a small positive charge from point Q to P will be negative.
  4. For the reasons as given in (iii), work done by an external agency in moving a small negative charge from B to A will be positive.
  5. As the potential energy of the negative charge increases, the kinetic energy of the negative charge decreases in going from point B to A.

subject: Physics
question_text: Four charges are arranged at the corners of a square ABCD of side d, as shown in fig. Find the work required to put together this arrangement. A charge q 0 is brought to the center E of the square, the four charges being held fixed at its corners. How much extra work is needed to do this?
question_html:
Four charges are arranged at the corners of a square ABCD of side d, as shown in fig. 

  1. Find the work required to put together this arrangement.
  2. A charge q0 is brought to the center E of the square, the four charges being held fixed at its corners. How much extra work is needed to do this?
solution_text: Since the work done depends on the final arrangement of the charges, and not on how they are put together, we calculate work needed for one way of putting the charges at A, B, C and D. Suppose, first the charge +q is brought to A, and then the charges –q, +q, and -q are brought to B, C, and D, respectively. The total work needed can be calculated in steps: Work needed to bring charge +q to A when no charge is present elsewhere: this is zero. Work needed to bring –q to B when +q is at A. This is given by (charge at B) {tex}\times{/tex} (electrostatic potential at B due to charge +q at A) = {tex}-q \times\left(\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}\right){/tex} = {tex}-\frac{q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}{/tex} Work needed to bring charge +q to C when +q is at A and -q is at B. This is given by (charge at C) {tex}\times{/tex} (potential at C due to charges at A and B = {tex}+q\left(\frac{+q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d \sqrt{2}}+\frac{-q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{-q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}\left(1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} Work needed to bring -q to D when +q at A, -q at B, and +q at C. This is given by (charge at D) {tex}\times{/tex} (potential at D due to charges at A, B, and C) = {tex}-q\left(\frac{+q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}+\frac{-q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d \sqrt{2}}+\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{-q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}\left(2-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} Add the work done in steps (i), (ii), (iii), and (iv). The total work required is = {tex}\frac{-q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}\left\{(0)+(1)+\left(1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+\left(2-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right\}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}(4-\sqrt{2}){/tex} The work done depends only on the arrangement of the charges, and not how they are assembled. By definition, this is the total electrostatic energy of the charges. The extra work necessary to bring a charge q 0 to point E when the four charges are at A, B, C, and D is q 0 {tex}\times{/tex} (electrostatic potential at E due to the charges at A, B, C, and D). The electrostatic potential at E is clearly zero since potential due to A and C is cancelled by that due to B and D. Hence, no work is required to bring any charge to point E. Also, it can be said that the work done over a closed surface is zero. (charges are opposite in corners so work done during one cycle cancel out by another cycle) hence work done is zero.
solution_html:
1. Since the work done depends on the final arrangement of the charges, and not on how they are put together, we calculate work needed for one way of putting the charges at A, B, C and D. Suppose, first the charge +q is brought to A, and then the charges –q, +q, and -q are brought to B, C, and D, respectively. The total work needed can be calculated in steps:
    1. Work needed to bring charge +q to A when no charge is present elsewhere: this is zero.
    2. Work needed to bring –q to B when +q is at A. This is given by (charge at B) {tex}\times{/tex} (electrostatic potential at B due to charge +q at A)
      = {tex}-q \times\left(\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}\right){/tex} = {tex}-\frac{q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}{/tex} 
    3. Work needed to bring charge +q to C when +q is at A and -q is at B. This is given by (charge at C) {tex}\times{/tex} (potential at C due to charges at A and B
      = {tex}+q\left(\frac{+q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d \sqrt{2}}+\frac{-q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}\right){/tex} 
      = {tex}\frac{-q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}\left(1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} 
    4. Work needed to bring -q to D when +q at A, -q at B, and +q at C. This is given by (charge at D) {tex}\times{/tex} (potential at D due to charges at A, B, and C)
      = {tex}-q\left(\frac{+q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}+\frac{-q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d \sqrt{2}}+\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}\right){/tex} 
      = {tex}\frac{-q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}\left(2-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} 
      Add the work done in steps (i), (ii), (iii), and (iv). The total work required is
      = {tex}\frac{-q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}\left\{(0)+(1)+\left(1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+\left(2-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right\}{/tex} 
      = {tex}\frac{-q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}(4-\sqrt{2}){/tex} 
      The work done depends only on the arrangement of the charges, and not how they are assembled. By definition, this is the total electrostatic energy of the charges.
  2. The extra work necessary to bring a charge q0 to point E when the four charges are at A, B, C, and D is q0 {tex}\times{/tex} (electrostatic potential at E due to the charges at A, B, C, and D). The electrostatic potential at E is clearly zero since potential due to A and C is cancelled by that due to B and D. Hence, no work is required to bring any charge to point E. Also, it can be said that the work done over a closed surface is zero. (charges are opposite in corners so work done during one cycle cancel out by another cycle) hence work done is zero.

subject: Physics
question_text: Determine the electrostatic potential energy of a system consisting of two charges 7 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and -2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C (and with no external field) placed at (-9 cm, 0, 0) and (9 cm, 0, 0) respectively. How much work is required to separate the two charges infinitely away from each other? Suppose that the same system of charges is now placed in an external electric field E = A ( {tex}\frac{1}{r^2}{/tex} ); A = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 NC -1 m 2 . What would the electrostatic energy of the configuration be?
question_html:
1. Determine the electrostatic potential energy of a system consisting of two charges 7 {tex}\mu{/tex}C and -2 {tex}\mu{/tex}C (and with no external field) placed at (-9 cm, 0, 0) and (9 cm, 0, 0) respectively.
  2. How much work is required to separate the two charges infinitely away from each other?
  3. Suppose that the same system of charges is now placed in an external electric field E = A ({tex}\frac{1}{r^2}{/tex}); A = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 NC-1 m2. What would the electrostatic energy of the configuration be?
solution_text: q 1 = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 C, q 2 = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 C, distance between the charges is, r = 9 - (-9) = 18 cm U = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r}{/tex} = {tex}9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{7 \times(-2) \times 10^{-12}}{0.18}{/tex} = -0.7 J W = U 2 - U 1 = 0 - U = 0 - (-0.7) = 0.7 J The mutual interaction energy of the two charges remains unchanged. In addition, there is the energy of interaction of the two charges with the external electric field. We find, q 1 V(r 1 ) + q 2 V(r 2 ) = {tex}A \frac{7 \mu C}{0.09 }+A \frac{-2 \mu C}{0.09 }{/tex} and the net electrostatic energy is q 1 V(r 1 ) + q 2 V(r 2 ) + {tex}\frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r_{12}}{/tex} = {tex}A \frac{7 \mu C}{0.09 }+A \frac{-2 \mu C}{0.09 }-0.7 J{/tex} = 70 - 20 - 0.7 = 49.3 J
solution_html:
1. q1 = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6C, q2 = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6C, distance between the charges is, r = 9 - (-9) = 18 cm
    U = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r}{/tex} = {tex}9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{7 \times(-2) \times 10^{-12}}{0.18}{/tex} = -0.7 J
  2. W = U2 - U1 = 0 - U = 0 - (-0.7) = 0.7 J
  3. The mutual interaction energy of the two charges remains unchanged. In addition, there is the energy of interaction of the two charges with the external electric field. We find,
    q1V(r1) + q2V(r2) = {tex}A \frac{7 \mu C}{0.09 }+A \frac{-2 \mu C}{0.09 }{/tex}  and the net electrostatic energy is 
    q1V(r1) + q2V(r2) + {tex}\frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r_{12}}{/tex} = {tex}A \frac{7 \mu C}{0.09 }+A \frac{-2 \mu C}{0.09 }-0.7 J{/tex} 
    = 70 - 20 - 0.7 = 49.3 J

subject: Physics
question_text: A molecule of a substance has permanent electric dipole moment equal to 10 -29 Cm. A mole of this substance is polarized (at low temperature) by applying a strong electrostatic field of magnitude 10 6 Vm -1 . The direction of the field is suddenly changed by an angle of 60 o . Estimate the heat released by the substance in aligning its dipoles along the new direction of the field. For simplicity, assume 100% polarization of the sample.
question_html: A molecule of a substance has permanent electric dipole moment equal to 10-29 Cm. A mole of this substance is polarized (at low temperature) by applying a strong electrostatic field of magnitude 106 Vm-1. The direction of the field is suddenly changed by an angle of 60o. Estimate the heat released by the substance in aligning its dipoles along the new direction of the field. For simplicity, assume 100% polarization of the sample.
solution_text: Here, strength of electrostatic field, E = 10 6 Vm -1 Electric dipole moment of a molecule, p = 10 -29 Cm The work done in rotating a dipole from its orientation making an angle {tex}\theta_1{/tex} with the direction of electric field to {tex}\theta_2{/tex} , W = pE (cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex} ) Here, {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 0 o and {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 60 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} W = 10 -29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 (cos 0 o - cos 60 o ) = 10 -23 (1 - 0.5) = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -24 J In one mole, there are molecules equal to Avogadro number N (= 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 23 mole -1 ). Therefore, total work done by the electric field on the dipoles in one mole of the substance.
solution_html:
Here, strength of electrostatic field, E = 106 Vm-1
Electric dipole moment of a molecule, p = 10-29 Cm
The work done in rotating a dipole from its orientation making an angle {tex}\theta_1{/tex} with the direction of electric field to {tex}\theta_2{/tex},
W = pE (cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex})
Here, {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 0o and {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 60o
{tex}\therefore{/tex} W = 10-29 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 (cos 0o - cos 60o) = 10-23 (1 - 0.5)
= 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24 J 
In one mole, there are molecules equal to Avogadro number N (= 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 mole-1).
Therefore, total work done by the electric field on the dipoles in one mole of the substance.

subject: Physics
question_text: A comb runs through one’s dry hair attracts small bits of paper. Why? What happens if the hair is wet or if it is a rainy day? (Remember, a paper does not conduct electricity.) Ordinary rubber is an insulator. But special rubber tyres of aircraft are made slightly conducting. Why is this necessary? Vehicles carrying inflammable materials usually have metallic ropes touching the ground during motion. Why? A bird perches on a bare high power line, and nothing happens to the bird. A man standing on the ground touches the same line and gets a fatal shock. Why?
question_html:
1. A comb runs through one’s dry hair attracts small bits of paper. Why? What happens if the hair is wet or if it is a rainy day? (Remember, a paper does not conduct electricity.)
  2. Ordinary rubber is an insulator. But special rubber tyres of aircraft are made slightly conducting. Why is this necessary?
  3. Vehicles carrying inflammable materials usually have metallic ropes touching the ground during motion. Why?
  4. A bird perches on a bare high power line, and nothing happens to the bird. A man standing on the ground touches the same line and gets a fatal shock. Why?
solution_text: This is because the comb gets electrically charged by friction when even through the dry hair. It attracts small bits of paper because, the molecules in the paper gets polarised by the charged comb, resulting in a net force of attraction. If the hair is wet, or if it is a rainy day, friction between hair and the comb reduces. The comb does not get charged and thus it will not attract small bits of paper. To enable them to conduct charge (produced by friction) to the ground; as too much of static electricity accumulated may result in spark and result in fire. To enable them to conduct charge (produced by friction) to the ground; as too much of static electricity accumulated may result in spark and result in fire. Current passes only when there is a difference in potential.
solution_html:
1. This is because the comb gets electrically charged by friction when even through the dry hair. It attracts small bits of paper because, the molecules in the paper gets polarised by the charged comb, resulting in a net force of attraction. If the hair is wet, or if it is a rainy day, friction between hair and the comb reduces. The comb does not get charged and thus it will not attract small bits of paper.
  2. To enable them to conduct charge (produced by friction) to the ground; as too much of static electricity accumulated may result in spark and result in fire.
  3. To enable them to conduct charge (produced by friction) to the ground; as too much of static electricity accumulated may result in spark and result in fire.
  4. Current passes only when there is a difference in potential.

subject: Physics
question_text: A slab of material of dielectric constant K has the same area as the plates of a parallel-plate capacitor but has a thickness ( {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} )d, where d is the separation of the plates. How is the capacitance changed when the slab is inserted between the plates?
question_html: A slab of material of dielectric constant K has the same area as the plates of a parallel-plate capacitor but has a thickness ({tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex})d, where d is the separation of the plates. How is the capacitance changed when the slab is inserted between the plates?
solution_text: Let E 0 = {tex}\frac{V_0}{d}{/tex} be the electric field between the plates when there is no dielectric and the potential difference is V 0 . If the dielectric is now inserted, the electric field in the dielectric will be E 0 = {tex}\frac{KV_0}{d} {/tex} .(having thickness 3/4 d) The potential difference when dielectric is introduced ,will then be V = {tex}E_{0}\left(\frac{1}{4} d\right)+\frac{E_{0}}{K}\left(\frac{3}{4} d\right){/tex} = {tex}E_{0} d\left(\frac{1}{4}+\frac{3}{4 K}\right){/tex} = {tex}V_{0} \frac{K+3}{4 K}{/tex} The potential difference decreases by the factor {tex}\frac{(K+3)}{4K}{/tex} while the free charge Q 0 on the plates remains unchanged. The capacitance thus increases C = {tex}\frac{Q_{0}}{V}=\frac{4 K}{K+3} \frac{Q_{0}}{V_{0}}=\frac{4 K}{K+3} C_{0}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E0 = {tex}\frac{V_0}{d}{/tex} be the electric field between the plates when there is no dielectric and the potential difference is V0. If the dielectric is now inserted, the electric field in the dielectric will be E0= {tex}\frac{KV_0}{d} {/tex}.(having thickness 3/4 d) The potential difference when dielectric is introduced ,will then be 
V = {tex}E_{0}\left(\frac{1}{4} d\right)+\frac{E_{0}}{K}\left(\frac{3}{4} d\right){/tex} 
= {tex}E_{0} d\left(\frac{1}{4}+\frac{3}{4 K}\right){/tex} = {tex}V_{0} \frac{K+3}{4 K}{/tex} 
The potential difference decreases by the factor {tex}\frac{(K+3)}{4K}{/tex} while the free charge Q0 on the plates remains unchanged. The capacitance thus increases
C = {tex}\frac{Q_{0}}{V}=\frac{4 K}{K+3} \frac{Q_{0}}{V_{0}}=\frac{4 K}{K+3} C_{0}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: A network of four 10 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitors is connected to a 500 V supply, as shown in fig. Determine the equivalent capacitance of the network and the charge on each capacitor. (Note, the charge on a capacitor is the charge on the plate with higher potential, equal and opposite to the charge on the plate with lower potential.)
question_html:
A network of four 10 {tex}\mu{/tex}F capacitors is connected to a 500 V supply, as shown in fig. Determine

  1. the equivalent capacitance of the network and
  2. the charge on each capacitor.
    (Note, the charge on a capacitor is the charge on the plate with higher potential, equal and opposite to the charge on the plate with lower potential.)
solution_text: In the given network, C 1 , C 2 and C 3 are connected in series.(effective capacitance is small as compared to individual capacitance) The effective capacitance C′ of these three capacitors is given by {tex}\frac{1}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}{/tex} For C 1 = C 2 = C 3 = 10 {tex}\mu{/tex} F, C′ = ( {tex}\frac{10}{3}{/tex} ) {tex}\mu{/tex} F. The network has C′ and C 4 connected in parallel. Thus, the equivalent capacitance C of the network is C = C' + C 4 = {tex}\left(\frac{10}{3}+10\right){/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} F = 13.3 {tex}\mu{/tex} F Clearly, from the figure, the charge on each of the capacitors, C 1 , C 2 and C 3 is the same, say Q. Let the charge on C 4 be Q′. Now, since the potential difference across AB is {tex}\frac{Q}{C_1}{/tex} , across BC is {tex}\frac{Q}{C_2}{/tex} , across CD is {tex}\frac{Q}{C_3}{/tex} , we have {tex}\frac{Q}{C_{1}}+\frac{Q}{C_{2}}+\frac{Q}{C_{3}}{/tex} = 500 V Also, {tex}\frac{Q'}{C_3}{/tex} = 500 V This gives for the given value of the capacitances. Q = {tex}500 V \times \frac{10}{3} \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 C and Q' = 500 V {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\mu{/tex} F = 5.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 C
solution_html:
1. In the given network, C1, C2 and C3 are connected in series.(effective capacitance is small as compared to individual capacitance) The effective capacitance C′ of these three capacitors is given by
    {tex}\frac{1}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}+\frac{1}{C_{3}}{/tex} 
    For C1 = C2 = C3 = 10 {tex}\mu{/tex}F, C′ = ({tex}\frac{10}{3}{/tex}) {tex}\mu{/tex}F. The network has C′ and C4 connected in parallel. Thus, the equivalent capacitance C of the network is
    C = C' + C4 = {tex}\left(\frac{10}{3}+10\right){/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex}F = 13.3{tex}\mu{/tex}F 
  2. Clearly, from the figure, the charge on each of the capacitors, C1, C2 and C3 is the same, say Q. Let the charge on C4 be Q′. Now, since the potential difference across AB is {tex}\frac{Q}{C_1}{/tex}, across BC is {tex}\frac{Q}{C_2}{/tex}, across CD is {tex}\frac{Q}{C_3}{/tex}, we have
    {tex}\frac{Q}{C_{1}}+\frac{Q}{C_{2}}+\frac{Q}{C_{3}}{/tex} = 500 V
    Also, {tex}\frac{Q'}{C_3}{/tex} = 500 V
    This gives for the given value of the capacitances.
    Q = {tex}500 V \times \frac{10}{3} \mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} = 1.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 C and Q' = 500 V {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\mu{/tex}F = 5.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 C

subject: Physics
question_text: A 900 pF capacitor is charged by 100 V battery [in a fig]. How much electrostatic energy is stored by the capacitor? The capacitor is disconnected from the battery and connected to another 900 pF capacitor [in a fig]. What is the electrostatic energy stored by the system?
question_html:
1. A 900 pF capacitor is charged by 100 V battery [in a fig]. How much electrostatic energy is stored by the capacitor?
  2. The capacitor is disconnected from the battery and connected to another 900 pF capacitor [in a fig]. What is the electrostatic energy stored by the system?
solution_text: V = 100V and C= 900 pF thus the charge on the capacitor is Q = CV = 900 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 F {tex}\times{/tex} 100 V = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 C The energy stored by the capacitor is = ( {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ) CV 2 = ( {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ) QV = ( {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ) {tex}\times{/tex} 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 C {tex}\times{/tex} 100 V = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 J Now the capacitor is disconnected and is connected to another 900 pF capacitor. In the steady situation, the two capacitors have their positive plates at the same potential and their negative plates at the same potential. Let the common potential difference be V′. The charge on each capacitor is then Q′ = CV′. By charge conservation, Q′ = {tex}\frac{Q}{2}{/tex} . This implies V′ = {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex} . The total energy of the system is = {tex}2 \times \frac{1}{2} {Q}^{\prime} V{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4} Q V{/tex} = 2.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 J Thus in going from (a) to (b), though no charge is lost; the final energy is only half the initial energy. There is a transient period before the system settles to the situation (b). During this period, a transient current flows from the first capacitor to the second. Energy is lost during this time in the form of heat and electromagnetic radiation.
solution_html:
1. V = 100V and C= 900 pF  thus the charge on the capacitor is
    Q = CV = 900 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 F {tex}\times{/tex} 100 V = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 C
    The energy stored by the capacitor is
    = ({tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}) CV2 = ({tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}) QV
    = ({tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}) {tex}\times{/tex} 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8C {tex}\times{/tex} 100 V = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 J

  2. Now the capacitor is disconnected and is connected to another 900 pF capacitor.
    In the steady situation, the two capacitors have their positive plates at the same potential and their negative plates at the same potential. Let the common potential difference be V′. The charge on each capacitor is then Q′ = CV′. By charge conservation, Q′ = {tex}\frac{Q}{2}{/tex}. This implies V′ = {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex}. The total energy of the system is = {tex}2 \times \frac{1}{2} {Q}^{\prime} V{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4} Q V{/tex} = 2.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 J

    Thus in going from (a) to (b), though no charge is lost; the final energy is only half the initial energy.
    There is a transient period before the system settles to the situation (b). During this period, a transient current flows from the first capacitor to the second. Energy is lost during this time in the form of heat and electromagnetic radiation.

subject: Physics
question_text: Two charges 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 C and -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 C are located 16 cm apart. At what point (s) on the line joining the two charges is the electrical potential zero? Take the potential at infinity to be zero.
question_html: Two charges 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 C and -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 C are located 16 cm apart. At what point (s) on the line joining the two charges is the electrical potential zero? Take the potential at infinity to be zero.
solution_text: Here q A = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 C; q B = -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 C ; r = 16 cm = 0.16 m Let O be the point, where the electric potential is zero due to the two charges as shown in figure given below. Suppose that the distance AO = x. Then BO = r - x = 0.16 - x Electric potential at point O due to q A , {tex}V_{A}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q_{A}}{A O}{/tex} {tex}=9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{5 \times 10^{-8}}{x}=\frac{450}{x}{/tex} Electric potential at point O due to q B , {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{BO}}{/tex} {tex}=9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{\left(-3 \times 10^{-8}\right)}{0 \cdot 16-x}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{270}{0.16-x}{/tex} Since the electric potential at point O is zero, we have {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}+\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=0{/tex} or {tex}\frac{450}{x}+\left(-\frac{270}{0 \cdot 16-x}\right)=0{/tex} or {tex}\frac{450}{x}=\frac{270}{0 \cdot 16-x}{/tex} or x = 0·1 m = 10 cm (from charge of 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 C)
solution_html:
Here qA = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 C; qB = -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 C ; r = 16 cm = 0.16 m
Let O be the point, where the electric potential is zero due to the two charges as shown in figure given below.

Suppose that the distance AO = x. Then
BO = r - x = 0.16 - x
Electric potential at point O due to qA,
{tex}V_{A}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q_{A}}{A O}{/tex}
{tex}=9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{5 \times 10^{-8}}{x}=\frac{450}{x}{/tex}
Electric potential at point O due to qB,
{tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{BO}}{/tex}
{tex}=9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{\left(-3 \times 10^{-8}\right)}{0 \cdot 16-x}{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{270}{0.16-x}{/tex}
Since the electric potential at point O is zero, we have
{tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}+\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=0{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{450}{x}+\left(-\frac{270}{0 \cdot 16-x}\right)=0{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{450}{x}=\frac{270}{0 \cdot 16-x}{/tex}
or x = 0·1 m = 10 cm (from charge of 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 C)

subject: Physics
question_text: A regular hexagon of side 10 cm has a charge {tex}5\mu C{/tex} at each of its vertices. Calculate the potential at the centre of the hexagon.
question_html: A regular hexagon of side 10 cm has a charge {tex}5\mu C{/tex} at each of its vertices. Calculate the potential at the centre of the hexagon.
solution_text: Given, {tex}q= 5 \times {10^{ - 6}}C{/tex} , r = 10 cm = 0.1 m Potential at the centre of the hexagon is given by {tex}V = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\left[ {\frac{q}{r} + \frac{q}{r} + \frac{q}{r} + \frac{q}{r} + \frac{q}{r} + \frac{q}{r}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = 6 \times \left( {\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}} \right)\frac{q}{r}{/tex} {tex}V = \frac{{6 \times 9 \times {{10}^9} \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{0.1}} = 2.7 \times {10^6}V{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, {tex}q= 5 \times {10^{ - 6}}C{/tex}, r = 10 cm = 0.1 m

Potential at the centre of the hexagon is given by
{tex}V = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\left[ {\frac{q}{r} + \frac{q}{r} + \frac{q}{r} + \frac{q}{r} + \frac{q}{r} + \frac{q}{r}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = 6 \times \left( {\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}} \right)\frac{q}{r}{/tex}
{tex}V = \frac{{6 \times 9 \times {{10}^9} \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{0.1}} = 2.7 \times {10^6}V{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: Two charges {tex}2\mu C{/tex} and {tex} - 2\mu C{/tex} are placed at points A and B, 6 cm apart. Identify an equipotential surface of the system. What is the direction of electric field at every point on this surface?
question_html:
Two charges {tex}2\mu C{/tex} and {tex} - 2\mu C{/tex} are placed at points A and B, 6 cm apart.

  1. Identify an equipotential surface of the system.
  2. What is the direction of electric field at every point on this surface?
solution_text: Given, {tex}q_1 = 2\mu C = 2 \times {10^{ - 6}}C{/tex} {tex}{q_2} = - 2\mu C = - 2 \times {10^{ - 6}}C{/tex} Potential will be zero due to both charges at equipotential surface. {tex}\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\left[ {\frac{{q_1}}{x} + \frac{{q_2}}{{(0.06 - x)}}} \right] = 0{/tex} or {tex}\frac{{q_1}}{x} = - \frac{{q_2}}{{(0.06 - x)}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{x} = \frac{{( - 2 \times {{10}^{ - 6}})}}{{\left[ {(0.06) - x} \right]}}{/tex} or x = 0.06 - x {tex}x = \frac{{0.06}}{2} = 0.03m{/tex} i.e. the plane normal to AB and passing through its mid point has zero potential everywhere. The direction of electric field is normal to the plane in the AB direction.
solution_html:
Given, {tex}q_1 = 2\mu C = 2 \times {10^{ - 6}}C{/tex}
{tex}{q_2} = - 2\mu C = - 2 \times {10^{ - 6}}C{/tex}

  1. Potential will be zero due to both charges at equipotential surface.

    {tex}\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\left[ {\frac{{q_1}}{x} + \frac{{q_2}}{{(0.06 - x)}}} \right] = 0{/tex}
    or {tex}\frac{{q_1}}{x} = - \frac{{q_2}}{{(0.06 - x)}}{/tex}
    or {tex}\frac{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{x} = \frac{{( - 2 \times {{10}^{ - 6}})}}{{\left[ {(0.06) - x} \right]}}{/tex}
    or x = 0.06 - x
    {tex}x = \frac{{0.06}}{2} = 0.03m{/tex}
    i.e. the plane normal to AB and passing through its mid point has zero potential everywhere.
  2. The direction of electric field is normal to the plane in the AB direction.

subject: Physics
question_text: A spherical conductor of radius 12 cm has a charge of {tex}1.6 \times {10^{ - 7}}C{/tex} distributed uniformly on its surface. What is the electric field inside the sphere? just outside the sphere? at a point 18 cm from the centre of the sphere?
question_html:
A spherical conductor of radius 12 cm has a charge of {tex}1.6 \times {10^{ - 7}}C{/tex} distributed uniformly on its surface.
What is the electric field

  1. inside the sphere?
  2. just outside the sphere?
  3. at a point 18 cm from the centre of the sphere?
solution_text: Given, {tex}q = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 7}}C{/tex} r = 0.12 m Electric field is zero inside the sphere because charge reside on outer surface of conductor. Electric field just outside the sphere is given by {tex}E = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{q}{{{r^2}}} = 9 \times {10^9} \times \frac{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{{{{(0.12)}^2}}}{/tex} = 10 5 NC -1 At a point 0.18 m from the centre of the sphere {tex} E = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{q}{{{r^2}}} = 9 \times {10^9} \times \frac{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{{{{(0.18)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = 4.4 \times {10^4}N{C^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, {tex}q = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 7}}C{/tex}
r = 0.12 m

  1. Electric field is zero inside the sphere because charge reside on outer surface of conductor.
  2. Electric field just outside the sphere is given by
    {tex}E = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{q}{{{r^2}}} = 9 \times {10^9} \times \frac{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{{{{(0.12)}^2}}}{/tex}
    = 105 NC-1
  3. At a point 0.18 m from the centre of the sphere
    {tex} E = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{q}{{{r^2}}} = 9 \times {10^9} \times \frac{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{{{{(0.18)}^2}}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 4.4 \times {10^4}N{C^{ - 1}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor with air between the plates has a capacitance of 8pF (1pF = 10 -12 F). What will be the capacitance if the distance between the plates is reduced by half, and the space between them is filled with a substance of dielectric constant 6?
question_html: A parallel plate capacitor with air between the plates has a capacitance of 8pF (1pF = 10-12F). What will be the capacitance if the distance between the plates is reduced by half, and the space between them is filled with a substance of dielectric constant 6?
solution_text: We know that, {tex}{C_0} = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d} = 8 \times {10^{ - 12}}{/tex} F If the distance is reduced to half i.e. d/2 and space between the plates is filled by a substance of k = 6. Then, {tex}C = \frac{{k{\varepsilon _0} \cdot A}}{{d/2}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2k({\varepsilon _0}A)}}{d}{/tex} {tex} = 2 \times 6 \times 8 \times {10^{ - 12}}{/tex} {tex} = 96 \times {10^{ - 12}}F{/tex} = 96 pF
solution_html:
We know that, {tex}{C_0} = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d} = 8 \times {10^{ - 12}}{/tex} F
If the distance is reduced to half i.e. d/2 and space between the plates is filled by a substance of k = 6.
Then, {tex}C = \frac{{k{\varepsilon _0} \cdot A}}{{d/2}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{2k({\varepsilon _0}A)}}{d}{/tex}
{tex} = 2 \times 6 \times 8 \times {10^{ - 12}}{/tex}
{tex} = 96 \times {10^{ - 12}}F{/tex} = 96 pF

subject: Physics
question_text: Three capacitors each of capacitance 9 pF are connected in series. What is the total capacitance of the combination? What is the potential difference across each capacitor if the combination is connected to a 120 V supply?
question_html:
Three capacitors each of capacitance 9 pF are connected in series.

  1. What is the total capacitance of the combination?
  2. What is the potential difference across each capacitor if the combination is connected to a 120 V supply?
solution_text: Given: C 1 = C 2 = C 3 = 9pF {tex} = 9 \times {10^{ - 12}}F{/tex} Since the capacitors are connected in series, {tex}\frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{{{C_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{C_2}}} + \frac{1}{{{C_3}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{{9 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}} + \frac{1}{{9 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}} + \frac{1}{{9 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{{{{10}^{ - 12}}}}\left[ {\frac{1}{9} + \frac{1}{9} + \frac{1}{9}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{{{10}^{ - 12}}}} \times \frac{3}{9} = \frac{1}{{3 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}}{/tex} {tex}C = 3 \times {10^{ 12}} = 3pF{/tex} Given, V = 120 volt V = V 1 + V 2 + V 3 As capacitance are equal, so V 1 = V 2 = V 3 = V' (let) V = 3V' {tex}V' = \frac{V}{3} = \frac{{120}}{3}\left( {\because {C_1} = {C_2} = {C_3} = C} \right){/tex} = 40 volt
solution_html:
Given: C1 = C2 = C3 = 9pF {tex} = 9 \times {10^{ - 12}}F{/tex}

  1. Since the capacitors are connected in series,
    {tex}\frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{{{C_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{C_2}}} + \frac{1}{{{C_3}}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{{9 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}} + \frac{1}{{9 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}} + \frac{1}{{9 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{1}{{{{10}^{ - 12}}}}\left[ {\frac{1}{9} + \frac{1}{9} + \frac{1}{9}} \right]{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac{1}{{{{10}^{ - 12}}}} \times \frac{3}{9} = \frac{1}{{3 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}}{/tex}
    {tex}C = 3 \times {10^{ 12}} = 3pF{/tex}
  2. Given, V = 120 volt
    V = V1 + V2 + V3
    As capacitance are equal, so V1 = V2 = V3 = V' (let)
    V = 3V'
    {tex}V' = \frac{V}{3} = \frac{{120}}{3}\left( {\because {C_1} = {C_2} = {C_3} = C} \right){/tex}
    = 40 volt

subject: Physics
question_text: Three capacitors of capacitances 2 pF, 3 pF and 4 pF are connected in parallel. What is the total capacitance of the combination? Determine the charge on each capacitor if the combination is connected to a 100 V supply.
question_html:
Three capacitors of capacitances 2 pF, 3 pF and 4 pF are connected in parallel.

  1. What is the total capacitance of the combination?
  2. Determine the charge on each capacitor if the combination is connected to a 100 V supply.
solution_text: Given, {tex}{C_1} = 2pF = 2 \times {10^{ - 12}}F{/tex} {tex}{C_2} = 3pF = 3 \times {10^{ - 12}}F{/tex} {tex}{C_3} = 4pF = 4 \times {10^{ - 12}}F{/tex} Since the capacitors are connected in parallel then C = C 1 + C 2 + C 3 {tex} = (2 + 3 + 4) \times {10^{ - 12}}{/tex} {tex} = 9 \times {10^{ - 12}} = 9pF{/tex} Given, V = 100 volt {tex}{q_1} = {C_1}V = 2 \times {10^{ - 12}} \times 100{/tex} {tex} = 2 \times {10^{ - 10}}C{/tex} {tex}{q_2} = {C_2}V = 3 \times {10^{ - 12}} \times 100{/tex} {tex} = 3 \times {10^{ - 10}}C{/tex} {tex}{q_3} = {C_3}V = 4 \times {10^{ - 12}} \times 100{/tex} {tex} = 4 \times {10^{ - 10}}C{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Given, {tex}{C_1} = 2pF = 2 \times {10^{ - 12}}F{/tex}
    {tex}{C_2} = 3pF = 3 \times {10^{ - 12}}F{/tex}
    {tex}{C_3} = 4pF = 4 \times {10^{ - 12}}F{/tex}
    Since the capacitors are connected in parallel then
    C = C1 + C2 + C3
    {tex} = (2 + 3 + 4) \times {10^{ - 12}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 9 \times {10^{ - 12}} = 9pF{/tex}
  2. Given, V = 100 volt
    {tex}{q_1} = {C_1}V = 2 \times {10^{ - 12}} \times 100{/tex}
    {tex} = 2 \times {10^{ - 10}}C{/tex}
    {tex}{q_2} = {C_2}V = 3 \times {10^{ - 12}} \times 100{/tex}
    {tex} = 3 \times {10^{ - 10}}C{/tex}
    {tex}{q_3} = {C_3}V = 4 \times {10^{ - 12}} \times 100{/tex}
    {tex} = 4 \times {10^{ - 10}}C{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: In a parallel plate capacitor with air between the plates, each plate has an area of {tex}6 \times {10^{ - 3}}{m^2}{/tex} and the distance between the plates is 3 mm. Calculate the capacitance of the capacitor. If this capacitor is connected to a 100 V supply, what is the charge on each plate of the capacitor?
question_html: In a parallel plate capacitor with air between the plates, each plate has an area of {tex}6 \times {10^{ - 3}}{m^2}{/tex} and the distance between the plates is 3 mm. Calculate the capacitance of the capacitor. If this capacitor is connected to a 100 V supply, what is the charge on each plate of the capacitor?
solution_text: Given, {tex}d = 3 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex} {tex}A = 6 \times {10^{ - 3}}{m^2}{/tex} {tex}C = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0} \cdot A}}{d} = \frac{{9 \times {{10}^{ - 12}} \times 6 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{3 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 18 \times {10^{ - 12}}F = 18\;pF{/tex} Here, V = 100V {tex}q = CV = 18 \times {10^{ - 12}} \times 100{/tex} {tex} = 1.8 \times {10^{ - 9}}C{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, {tex}d = 3 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex}
{tex}A = 6 \times {10^{ - 3}}{m^2}{/tex}
{tex}C = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0} \cdot A}}{d} = \frac{{9 \times {{10}^{ - 12}} \times 6 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{3 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{/tex}
{tex} = 18 \times {10^{ - 12}}F = 18\;pF{/tex}
Here, V = 100V
{tex}q = CV = 18 \times {10^{ - 12}} \times 100{/tex}
{tex} = 1.8 \times {10^{ - 9}}C{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: Explain what would happen if in the capacitor of 17.7 pF, a 3 mm thick mica sheet of electric constant = 6 were inserted between the plates while the voltage supply remained connected. after the supply was disconnected.
question_html:
Explain what would happen if in the capacitor of 17.7 pF, a 3 mm thick mica sheet of electric constant = 6 were inserted between the plates

  1. while the voltage supply remained connected.
  2. after the supply was disconnected.
solution_text: When the voltage supply remains connected: The capacitance of the capacitor will become K times. Therefore, C’ = kC Where k = dielectric constant Thus, C' = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 17.7pF = 106.2 pF The potential difference across the two plates of the capacitor will remain equal to the supply voltage i.e. 100 V The charge on the capacitor, q’ = C’V = 106.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 1.062 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 C After the voltage supply is disconnected: As calculated above, the capacitance of the capacitor, C’ = 106.2 pF The potential difference will decrease on introducing mica sheet by a factor of K, {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{V'}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{100}{6}=16.67 \mathrm{V}{/tex} The charge on the capacitor., q' = C'V' = 106.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 16.67 q' = 1.77 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 C
solution_html:
1. When the voltage supply remains connected:
    The capacitance of the capacitor will become K times.
    Therefore, C’ = kC
    Where k = dielectric constant
    Thus, C' = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 17.7pF = 106.2 pF
    The potential difference across the two plates of the capacitor will remain equal to the supply voltage i.e. 100 V
    The charge on the capacitor,
    q’ = C’V = 106.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 {tex}\times{/tex} 100
    = 1.062 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 C
  2. After the voltage supply is disconnected:
    As calculated above, the capacitance of the capacitor, C’ = 106.2 pF
    The potential difference will decrease on introducing mica sheet by a factor of K,
    {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{V'}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{100}{6}=16.67 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
    The charge on the capacitor.,
    q' = C'V' = 106.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 {tex}\times{/tex} 16.67
    q' = 1.77 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9C

subject: Physics
question_text: A 12 pF capacitor is connected to a 50 V battery. How much electrostatic energy is stored in the capacitor?
question_html: A 12 pF capacitor is connected to a 50 V battery. How much electrostatic energy is stored in the capacitor?
solution_text: Given, V = 50 V C = 12 pF {tex} = 12 \times {10^{ - 12}}F{/tex} Electrostatic energy stored in the capacitor is given as {tex}U = \frac{1}{2}C{V^2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times {10^{ - 12}} \times {(50)^2}{/tex} {tex}U = 1.5 \times {10^{ - 8}}J{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, V = 50 V
C = 12 pF {tex} = 12 \times {10^{ - 12}}F{/tex}
Electrostatic energy stored in the capacitor is given as
{tex}U = \frac{1}{2}C{V^2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times {10^{ - 12}} \times {(50)^2}{/tex}
{tex}U = 1.5 \times {10^{ - 8}}J{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: A 600 pF capacitor is charged by a 200 V supply. It is then disconnected from the supply and is connected to another uncharged 600 pF capacitor. How much electrostatic energy is lost in the process?
question_html: A 600 pF capacitor is charged by a 200 V supply. It is then disconnected from the supply and is connected to another uncharged 600 pF capacitor. How much electrostatic energy is lost in the process?
solution_text: Here C 1 = 600 pF, V 1 = 200 V, C 2 = 600 pF, V 2 =0 Common potential, {tex}V=\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}=\frac{600 \times 10^{-12} \times 200+0}{(600+600) \times 10^{-12}}=100 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} Initial energy stored, U i = U 1 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} C 1 V 1 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 600 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} (200) 2 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 J Final energy stored, Uf = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (C 1 + C 2 ) V 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (600+600) {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} (100) 2 = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 J Electrostatic energy lost, {tex}\Delta{/tex} U = U i -U f = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 J
solution_html:
Here C1 = 600 pF, V1 = 200 V,
C2 = 600 pF, V2 =0
Common potential,
{tex}V=\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}=\frac{600 \times 10^{-12} \times 200+0}{(600+600) \times 10^{-12}}=100 \mathrm{~V}{/tex}
Initial energy stored,
Ui = U1 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} C1V12  = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 600 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 {tex}\times{/tex} (200)2 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 J
Final energy stored,
Uf = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(C1 + C2) V2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (600+600) {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 {tex}\times{/tex}(100)2= 6 {tex}\times{/tex}10-6 J
Electrostatic energy lost,
{tex}\Delta{/tex} U = Ui -Uf = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 J
